# SETCO

## The Systematic Epistemic Theological Computational Ontology

### A Binary Quadratic-Framed Geometric Topology Decoupling Boundary, Relation, and Agreement for Projective Consensus

**Byline:**
A pedagogical construction of the Omnicron-facing interpretation of the Omi Object Model for presenting canonical authorship of the hexadecimal-encoded, algorithmic, deterministic ***omi---imo?O_o*** encapsulation notation.

**Encompassing the Integral Interpretive Layers:**

```text
Omi Object Model
 the root object/address model and canonical notation base

Omicron
 the embedded deterministic closure and normalization axis

Metatron
 the embedded transposition operator and transcription-resolution axis

Tetragrammatron
 the embedded fourfold quadratic governor of boundary-preserving relation

Omnicron
 the user-facing resolver interface exposing Omicron, Metatron, and Tetragrammatron through an impotent and/or innocuous projection and interpretation surface
```

In SETCO, Omicron, Metatron, and Tetragrammatron are embedded construction layers. They are not the ordinary user interface.

Users interface with Omnicron.

Omnicron presents the embedded layers as readable, inspectable, and interpretable projections without collapsing them into one authority and without requiring the user to directly operate the underlying closure, transposition, or quadratic-governor machinery.

The base remains the Omi Object Model. Omnicron is the resolver-facing interface to its embedded interpretive construction layers.
:::

The key phrase I would carry forward is:

```text
Users interface with Omnicron; Omnicron exposes the embedded Omicron, Metatron, and Tetragrammatron layers as impotent or innocuous projections.
```

# SETCO

## The Systematic Epistemic Theological Computational Ontology

### A Binary Quadratic-Framed Geometric Topology Decoupling Boundary, Relation, and Agreement for Projective Consensus

**Byline:**
A pedagogical construction of the Omnicron-facing interpretation of the Omi Object Model for presenting canonical authorship of the hexadecimal-encoded, algorithmic, deterministic ***omi---imo?O_o*** encapsulation notation.

**Encompassing the Integral Interpretive Layers:**

```text
Omi Object Model
 the root object/address model and canonical notation base

Omicron
 the embedded deterministic closure and normalization layer

Metatron
 the embedded transposition operator and transcription-resolution layer

Tetragrammatron
 the embedded fourfold quadratic governor of boundary-preserving relation

omi-notation
 the canonical interface notation through which Omicron, Metatron, and Tetragrammatron are addressed

Omnicron
 the user-facing resolver interface exposing OMI notation through impotent and/or innocuous projection surfaces

omi-lisp surface face
 the local declaration surface for readable construction

omi-port surface face
 the external reference surface for bit-board, bit-blit, adapter, and port-oriented projections
```

In SETCO, Omicron, Metatron, and Tetragrammatron are embedded construction layers. They are interfaced through `omi-notation`, not directly operated as ordinary user surfaces.

Omnicron is the user-facing resolver that presents those embedded layers through selectable surface faces. These faces may be local, as in the `omi-lisp` surface, or external/adaptive, as in the `omi-port` surface.

The projection remains impotent or innocuous until a separate implementation layer promotes it into action. SETCO therefore preserves the distinction between notation, projection, interface, and authority.
:::

The key lock is:

```text
We interface with Omicron, Metatron, and Tetragrammatron through omi-notation.
Omnicron exposes that notation through surface faces.
```

That lets **surface** come back in the right place: not as the deep authority layer, but as the reachable presentation face.

:::writing{variant="document" id="92764"}
# SETCO

## The Systematic Epistemic Theological Computational Ontology

### A Binary Quadratic-Framed Geometric Topology Decoupling Boundary, Relation, and Agreement for Projective Consensus

**Byline:**
A pedagogical construction of the Omnicron-facing interpretation of the Omi Object Model for presenting canonical authorship of the hexadecimal-encoded, algorithmic, deterministic ***omi---imo?O_o*** encapsulation notation.

---

## Foundational Frame

SETCO uses the binary quadratic frame:

```text
Q(x,y) = 60x² + 16xy + 4y²
```

as the organizing form for a geometric topology of relation.

The topology is geometric because relation may be projected into shapes, surfaces, faces, incidence fields, and readable coordinate structures.

The topology is decoupled because Boundary, Relation, Agreement, and Projection are not collapsed into one authority.

```text
Boundary ≠ Relation
Relation ≠ Agreement
Agreement ≠ Projection
Projection ≠ Authority
Consensus ≠ Collapse
```

Projective Consensus means bounded views may touch, align, and become mutually inspectable without becoming the same view.

---

## Naming Authority

The base remains the **Omi Object Model**.

The **Omnicron-facing interpretation** is a pedagogical presentation layer over the Omi Object Model. It does not replace OMI, and it does not collapse Omicron, Omnicron, Metatron, Tetragrammatron, or the Omi-* extension faces into one authority.

```text
Omi Object Model
 the root object/address model and canonical notation base

Omnicron-facing interpretation
 the pedagogical resolver-facing presentation of OMI

omi-* extension faces
 adaptive interface configurations that speak OMI notation
```

Approved wording:

```text
SETCO is a pedagogical construction of the Omnicron-facing interpretation of the Omi Object Model.
```

Prohibited wording:

```text
The Omnicron Object Model replaces the Omi Object Model.
```

---

## Integral Interpretive Layers

```text
Omi Object Model
 the root object/address model and canonical notation base

Omicron
 the embedded deterministic closure and normalization layer

Metatron
 the embedded transposition operator and transcription-resolution layer

Tetragrammatron
 the embedded fourfold quadratic governor of boundary-preserving relation

omi-notation
 the canonical interface notation through which Omicron, Metatron, and Tetragrammatron are addressed

Omnicron
 the user-facing resolver interface exposing OMI notation through impotent and/or innocuous projection surfaces

omi-lisp surface face
 the local declaration surface for readable construction

omi-port surface face
 the external reference surface for bit-board, bit-blit, adapter, and port-oriented projections
```

In SETCO, Omicron, Metatron, and Tetragrammatron are embedded construction layers. They are interfaced through `omi-notation`, not directly operated as ordinary user surfaces.

Omnicron is the user-facing resolver that presents those embedded layers through selectable surface faces. These faces may be local, as in the `omi-lisp` surface, or external/adaptive, as in the `omi-port` surface.

The projection remains impotent or innocuous until a separate implementation layer promotes it into action.

---

## SETCO Dimensional Interface Ladder

```text
Point:
 Omicron

Line:
 Metatron

Plane:
 Tetragrammatron

Space:
 Omnicron

HyperSpace:
 Omi-* termed interface configurations
```

### Dimensional Reading

```text
Omicron
 Point
 the embedded deterministic closure and normalization locus

Metatron
 Line
 the transposition operator joining, carrying, and resolving transcription across points

Tetragrammatron
 Plane
 the fourfold quadratic governor that preserves boundary-bearing relation across a projected surface

Omnicron
 Space
 the user-facing resolver space that exposes OMI notation through impotent and/or innocuous projection surfaces

Omi-* Interface Configurations
 HyperSpace
 adaptive interface configurations formed from omi-lisp declarations, omi-port declarations, raw binary bit-boards, bit-blotter references, and Omnicron runtime-resolved definitions
```

Canonical lock:

```text
Point → Omicron
Line → Metatron
Plane → Tetragrammatron
Space → Omnicron
HyperSpace → Omi-* configured interface surfaces
```

The user interfaces with Omnicron as space.

Omnicron may expose embedded Omicron, Metatron, and Tetragrammatron layers through `omi-notation`, while Omi-* hyperspace configurations extend that resolver space through local `omi-lisp` surfaces, external `omi-port` surfaces, raw binary bit-boards, and bit-blotter references.

These hyperspace configurations may resolve runtime definitions, but they do not replace the base authority of the Omi Object Model.

---

## Projection Authority Boundary

SETCO presents names as interpretive construction layers. It does not collapse them into one authority.

```text
Omicron
 closes and normalizes

Metatron
 transposes and resolves transcription

Tetragrammatron
 governs fourfold quadratic relation

Omnicron
 exposes the resolver-facing interface

Omi-* faces
 configure adaptive projection surfaces
```

Projection is not authority.

Notation is not execution.

Interpretation is not acceptance.

Consensus is not collapse.

The base remains OMI. Omnicron is the resolver-facing interface. Omi-* configurations are adaptive hyperspace faces over the Omi Object Model.
:::

This is now strong enough to use as the front matter / opening canon block.

---

`2026-07-12 22:41` | `id:260712-2241` ^260712-224152

Yes — that is the better interface rule.

The correction is:

```text id="frdupk"
Do not say:

Say:
 They are interfaced through Omnicron, using omi-notation, omi-lisp, or omi-port surfaces.
```

Because **Omnicron is the resolver-facing interface**, and the surface used depends on context:

```text id="4tpdr3"
omi-notation
```

Here is the corrected integrated block:

:::writing{variant="document" id="49728"}
## Interface Rule

In SETCO, Omicron, Metatron, and Tetragrammatron are embedded construction layers.

They are interfaced through **Omnicron**.

Omnicron may expose those embedded layers through one or more OMI-compatible surfaces:

```text id="9e38ua"
omi-notation
 the direct canonical notation surface

omi-lisp
 the local declaration surface

omi-port
 the external/adaptive port surface
```

When no previous address is available, Omnicron may return the empty seed:

```text id="uhrj5z"
(NULL . NULL)
```

This behaves like the Lisp empty pair/root seed: it does not mean failure, absence of identity, or invalidity. It means there is no prior address to provide, so the resolver returns the canonical empty starting point.

Therefore, the corrected interface chain is:

```text id="tfajnc"
User
 → Omnicron
 → omi-notation / omi-lisp / omi-port
 → Omicron / Metatron / Tetragrammatron
```

Omnicron is the user-facing resolver interface. `omi-notation`, `omi-lisp`, and `omi-port` are surface faces through which Omnicron presents or requests OMI-compatible structure.

The embedded layers remain embedded. The user does not directly operate Omicron, Metatron, or Tetragrammatron as separate application surfaces.
:::

And the sentence to replace in the big block should become:

```text id="81grfu"
In SETCO, Omicron, Metatron, and Tetragrammatron are embedded construction layers. They are interfaced through Omnicron, which may expose or request structure through omi-notation, omi-lisp, or omi-port surfaces. When no prior address is available, Omnicron returns the canonical empty seed `(NULL . NULL)`.
```

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-12 22:52` | `id:260712-2252` ^260712-225236

Yes — that is the clean interface rule.

The **Omicron Null Byte Omi-Ring** is the embedded deterministic closure ring:

```text
((0x00 . 0x20)
 (0x20 . 0x7F)
 (0x7F . 0xFF)
 (0xFF . 0x00))
```

When Omnicron interfaces with that embedded ring, it does not expose the raw ring directly as the user surface. It projects it into a gauge ring surface.

So the return surface may be either:

```text
Null Gauge Omi-Ring:
F0 00 1C 1D 1E 1F 20 F0
```

or:

```text
Gnomic Gauge Omi-Ring:
FΔ 00 1C 1D 1E 1F 20 FΔ
```

The distinction is:

```text
Omicron Null Byte Omi-Ring
 embedded deterministic closure ring

Null Gauge Omi-Ring
 canonical empty/null projection surface

Gnomic Gauge Omi-Ring
 declared interpretive/projection surface with a changeable gauge head/tail
```

I would lock it like this:

:::writing{variant="document" id="13068"}
## Null Ring Interface Rule

The embedded Omicron closure ring is the Null Byte Omi-Ring:

```text
((0x00 . 0x20)
 (0x20 . 0x7F)
 (0x7F . 0xFF)
 (0xFF . 0x00))
```

This ring is not ordinarily exposed directly as the user-facing surface.

When Omnicron interfaces with the Omicron Null Byte Omi-Ring, it projects the result into a gauge-level Omi-Ring surface.

If no prior address, declaration, or local interpretive gauge is available, Omnicron returns the canonical Null Gauge Omi-Ring:

```text
F0 00 1C 1D 1E 1F 20 F0
```

If a declared interpretive gauge is available, Omnicron may return a Gnomic Gauge Omi-Ring:

```text
FΔ 00 1C 1D 1E 1F 20 FΔ
```

Therefore:

```text
Omicron Null Byte Omi-Ring
 → Omnicron resolver interface
 → Null Gauge Omi-Ring
 or
 → Gnomic Gauge Omi-Ring
```

The Null Gauge Omi-Ring is the empty canonical projection.

The Gnomic Gauge Omi-Ring is the declared interpretive projection.

Both are projection surfaces. Neither collapses the embedded Omicron closure ring into user authority.
:::

Compact canon:

```text
Omicron closes the null-byte ring.
Omnicron projects the closure into a gauge ring.
F0 returns the null gauge.
FΔ returns the declared gnomic gauge.
```

# SETCO

## The Systematic Epistemic Theological Computational Ontology

### A Binary Quadratic-Framed Geometric Topology Decoupling Boundary, Relation, and Agreement for Projective Consensus

**Byline:**
A pedagogical construction of the Omnicron-facing interpretation of the Omi Object Model for presenting canonical authorship of the hexadecimal-encoded, algorithmic, deterministic ***omi---imo?O_o*** encapsulation notation.

---

## Foundational Frame

SETCO uses the binary quadratic frame:

```text
Q(x,y) = 60x² + 16xy + 4y²
```

as the organizing form for a geometric topology of relation.

The topology is geometric because relation may be projected into shapes, surfaces, faces, incidence fields, and readable coordinate structures.

The topology is decoupled because Boundary, Relation, Agreement, and Projection are not collapsed into one authority.

```text
Boundary ≠ Relation
Relation ≠ Agreement
Agreement ≠ Projection
Projection ≠ Authority
Consensus ≠ Collapse
```

Projective Consensus means bounded views may touch, align, and become mutually inspectable without becoming the same view.

---

## Naming Authority

The base remains the **Omi Object Model**.

The **Omnicron-facing interpretation** is a pedagogical presentation layer over the Omi Object Model. It does not replace OMI, and it does not collapse Omicron, Omnicron, Metatron, Tetragrammatron, or the Omi-* extension faces into one authority.

```text
Omi Object Model
 the root object/address model and canonical notation base

Omnicron-facing interpretation
 the pedagogical resolver-facing presentation of OMI

omi-* extension faces
 adaptive interface configurations that speak OMI notation
```

Approved wording:

```text
SETCO is a pedagogical construction of the Omnicron-facing interpretation of the Omi Object Model.
```

Prohibited wording:

```text
The Omnicron Object Model replaces the Omi Object Model.
```

---

## Integral Interpretive Layers

```text
Omi Object Model
 the root object/address model and canonical notation base

Omicron
 the embedded deterministic closure and normalization layer

Metatron
 the embedded transposition operator and transcription-resolution layer

Tetragrammatron
 the embedded fourfold quadratic governor of boundary-preserving relation

Omnicron
 the user-facing resolver interface exposing OMI-compatible structure through impotent and/or innocuous projection surfaces

omi-notation
 the direct canonical notation surface

omi-lisp
 the local declaration surface

omi-port
 the external/adaptive port surface
```

In SETCO, Omicron, Metatron, and Tetragrammatron are embedded construction layers.

They are interfaced through **Omnicron**, which may expose or request structure through `omi-notation`, `omi-lisp`, or `omi-port` surfaces.

When no prior address is available, Omnicron returns the canonical empty seed:

```text
(NULL . NULL)
```

This does not mean failure, absence of identity, or invalidity. It means there is no prior address to provide, so the resolver returns the canonical empty starting point.

Corrected interface chain:

```text
User
 → Omnicron
 → omi-notation / omi-lisp / omi-port
 → Omicron / Metatron / Tetragrammatron
```

The embedded layers remain embedded. The user does not directly operate Omicron, Metatron, or Tetragrammatron as separate application surfaces.

---

## SETCO Dimensional Interface Ladder

```text
Point:
 Omicron

Line:
 Metatron

Plane:
 Tetragrammatron

Space:
 Omnicron

HyperSpace:
 Omi-* termed interface configurations
```

### Dimensional Reading

```text
Omicron
 Point
 the embedded deterministic closure and normalization locus

Metatron
 Line
 the transposition operator joining, carrying, and resolving transcription across points

Tetragrammatron
 Plane
 the fourfold quadratic governor that preserves boundary-bearing relation across a projected surface

Omnicron
 Space
 the user-facing resolver space that exposes OMI notation through impotent and/or innocuous projection surfaces

Omi-* Interface Configurations
 HyperSpace
 adaptive interface configurations formed from omi-lisp declarations, omi-port declarations, raw binary bit-boards, bit-blotter references, and Omnicron runtime-resolved definitions
```

Canonical lock:

```text
Point → Omicron
Line → Metatron
Plane → Tetragrammatron
Space → Omnicron
HyperSpace → Omi-* configured interface surfaces
```

The user interfaces with Omnicron as space.

Omnicron may expose embedded Omicron, Metatron, and Tetragrammatron layers through OMI-compatible surfaces, while Omi-* hyperspace configurations extend that resolver space through local `omi-lisp` surfaces, external `omi-port` surfaces, raw binary bit-boards, and bit-blotter references.

These hyperspace configurations may resolve runtime definitions, but they do not replace the base authority of the Omi Object Model.

---

## Null Ring Interface Rule

The embedded Omicron closure ring is the Null Byte Omi-Ring:

```text
((0x00 . 0x20)
 (0x20 . 0x7F)
 (0x7F . 0xFF)
 (0xFF . 0x00))
```

This ring is not ordinarily exposed directly as the user-facing surface.

When Omnicron interfaces with the Omicron Null Byte Omi-Ring, it projects the result into a gauge-level Omi-Ring surface.

If no prior address, declaration, or local interpretive gauge is available, Omnicron returns the canonical Null Gauge Omi-Ring:

```text
F0 00 1C 1D 1E 1F 20 F0
```

If a declared interpretive gauge is available, Omnicron may return a Gnomic Gauge Omi-Ring:

```text
FΔ 00 1C 1D 1E 1F 20 FΔ
```

Therefore:

```text
Omicron Null Byte Omi-Ring
 → Omnicron resolver interface
 → Null Gauge Omi-Ring
 or
 → Gnomic Gauge Omi-Ring
```

The Null Gauge Omi-Ring is the empty canonical projection.

The Gnomic Gauge Omi-Ring is the declared interpretive projection.

Both are projection surfaces. Neither collapses the embedded Omicron closure ring into user authority.

Compact canon:

```text
Omicron closes the null-byte ring.
Omnicron projects the closure into a gauge ring.
F0 returns the null gauge.
FΔ returns the declared gnomic gauge.
```

---

## Projection Authority Boundary

SETCO presents names as interpretive construction layers. It does not collapse them into one authority.

```text
Omicron
 closes and normalizes

Metatron
 transposes and resolves transcription

Tetragrammatron
 governs fourfold quadratic relation

Omnicron
 exposes the resolver-facing interface

Omi-* faces
 configure adaptive projection surfaces
```

Projection is not authority.

Notation is not execution.

Interpretation is not acceptance.

Consensus is not collapse.

The base remains OMI. Omnicron is the resolver-facing interface. Omi-* configurations are adaptive hyperspace faces over the Omi Object Model.
:::

This is now the coherent canon section with the naming boundary, interface rule, dimensional ladder, and null/gauge return rule all aligned.

---

The file you just attached already supports this direction: it says the research model separates **rules** from **algorithms**, where rules answer “what do we know, not know, suspect, or need to discover?” and algorithms answer “what can be computed, implemented, checked, or proved?” filecite turn2file0 It also gives the canvas edge model: **Top = UU, Right = KU, Bottom = KK, Left = UK**, with each side functioning as a research orientation around a current checkpoint filecite turn2file0 .

So yes: this is basically your research method, but made architectural.

I would frame it like this:

:::writing{variant="document" id="30984"}
## SETCO as an External Development Frame Buffer

SETCO functions as an external development frame buffer for OMI research.

It does not execute the system directly. It holds projected research state outside the runtime so that Omi-* faces can be developed, inspected, compared, and refined before being resolved through Omnicron.

```text
SETCO:
 external epistemic frame buffer

Omi-Canvas:
 spatial research surface

Omi-* faces:
 tetrahedral development frames

Omnicron:
 resolver-facing interface that reads, projects, and exposes those faces
```

Each Omi-* face may be treated as a tetrahedral frame with four research orientations:

```text
Bottom:
 KK / Known Known / foundation

Right:
 KU / Known Unknown / explicit next question

Left:
 UK / Unknown Known / latent archive or unintegrated artifact

Top:
 UU / Unknown Unknown / frontier above the current map
```

This gives each Omi-* face a stable external working geometry:

```text
 UU
 ↑
UK ← Omi-* Face → KU
 ↓
 KK
```

The face itself is the current checkpoint.

The bottom edge stores what is already stable.
The right edge names the next stated research gap.
The left edge retrieves older or latent material not yet integrated.
The top edge preserves the frontier that is not yet expressible as a question.

This makes SETCO similar to a software-development frame buffer: it is an external surface where state can be staged, inspected, redrawn, and compared before being committed into implementation.

But unlike an ordinary graphics frame buffer, SETCO buffers epistemic and algorithmic state:

```text
rules:
 epistemic research objects

algorithms:
 implementation execution objects
```

Rules carry research modality:

```text
KK / KU / UK / UU
```

Algorithms carry implementation status:

```text
proved / implemented / defined_model / interpretation
```

The tetrahedral frame prevents these from collapsing into one another.

An Omi-* face therefore becomes a development frame for Omnicron:

```text
Omi-* Face
 → staged in SETCO / Omi-Canvas
 → classified by epistemic edge
 → assigned a face focus
 → resolved through Omnicron
 → exposed through omi-notation, omi-lisp, or omi-port
```

The face focus may be:

```text
cardinality
chirality
ordinality
functionality
```

Together, these form a Pascal-pyramid-like assurance model: foundation at the base, explicit questions as ladders, latent archive knowledge as side support, and unknown unknowns above the current map.

In this sense, SETCO is your research method formalized as a development architecture.

It is the external frame buffer where Omi-* faces are drawn before Omnicron resolves them into usable projection surfaces.
:::

The phrase I would lock is:

```text
SETCO is an external epistemic frame buffer for developing Omi-* faces as tetrahedral research frames resolved through Omnicron.
```

That captures it cleanly without making SETCO an execution engine.

\# Canonization & Adjudication

\## External Pattern — How to Classify Claims Before Using Them

\### 1. Purpose

This document describes the canonization and adjudication method as an **external, reusable pattern**. It strips away OMI-specific machinery (Omi-Ring, Icosian units, E8 roots, Fano planes, etc.) to expose the pure classification structure that any project can adopt.

\### 2. The Core Insight

Before using a claim in a deterministic system, you need answers to two questions:

1. **Canonization**: "What structure has been proven enough to admit into deterministic construction?"
2. **Adjudication**: "What is this claim permitted to do, and what are its failure boundaries?"

These questions are independent of the specific domain. Any project that mixes formal proof, informal reasoning, and implementation can benefit from making them explicit.

\### 3. Canonization Pattern

A concept may be canonized only if it can be rewritten as one of:

```
finite structure — decidable, enumerable
algebraic structure — operations, laws, morphisms
order structure — partial/total order, lattice
closure structure — closure operator, fixed points
boundary structure — d² = 0 invariant
quotient structure — equivalence, canonical representatives
duality structure — chain/cochain, construction/evaluation
decidable predicate — algorithmically decidable
deterministic transition — function, not relation
norm-preserving action — isometry, invariant
```

If a concept cannot be rewritten this way, it remains outside the canonization boundary. It may still be useful as a metaphor or research note, but it is not admissible for deterministic construction.

\### 4. Adjudication Pattern

Every claim must be classified by proof status level:

| Level | Name | Meaning |
|-------|------|---------|
| **P0** | Formally proven | Fully verified in a proof assistant. No axioms, no gaps. |
| **P1** | Exhaustively checked | Verified by finite computation, bounded search, or decision procedure. |
| **P2** | Mathematically modeled | Stated formally with an informal proof. Not machine-checked. |
| **P3** | Implementation precedent | Works in practice (tested). Not yet connected to formal spine. |
| **P4** | Metaphor / visualization | Useful for explanation only. Not proof. |

\#### Use Rules

| Level | Core use | Guide architecture | Guide implementation | Guide explanation |
|-------|----------|-------------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| P0 | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| P1 | ⚠️ Selected only | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| P2 | ❌ | ✅ | ⚠️ | ✅ |
| P3 | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| P4 | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |

\### 5. Claim Registry

Every admitted claim must be recorded with:

```
Claim ID — unique identifier
Name — human-readable
Source — origin
Status — P0–P4
Statement — formal statement
Module/File — where it lives
Dependencies — prerequisite claims
Allowed use — what it may be used for
Forbidden use — what it must NOT be used for
Failure boundary — what it does NOT prove
Promotion path — how to raise its status
```

\### 6. Authority Boundary

This method does not create a new authority layer. The boundary is:

```
Proof canonizes admissible structure.
Validation determines accepted state.
Attestation witnesses accepted transition.
Projection displays accepted state.
```

Proof tells the system what structures it is allowed to trust as deterministic. Validation decides whether a specific candidate is accepted.

\### 7. Adapting the Pattern

To adopt this pattern in another project:

1. Define your P0–P4 levels (or an equivalent status ladder).
2. Define your canonization criteria (what structures are admissible).
3. Create a claim registry for every concept you use.
4. Annotate each use of a claim with its permitted level.
5. Record failure boundaries explicitly.

That is the entire method. The rest is domain-specific filling.

:::writing{variant="document" id="40683"}
# Canonization & Adjudication

## External Pattern for Classifying Claims Before Use

### Purpose

Canonization and adjudication form the external reusable method behind SETCO.

This pattern strips away OMI-specific machinery such as Omi-Rings, Omnicron framing, Tetragrammatron validation, Metatron interpolation, E8 roots, Fano planes, and binary gauge notation. What remains is the general classification structure any project can use before admitting claims into deterministic construction.

SETCO uses this method as its epistemic frame buffer.

OMI is one application of the method.

---

## Core Insight

Before a claim may be used inside a deterministic system, two questions must be answered:

```text
Canonization:
 What structure has been proven enough to admit into deterministic construction?

Adjudication:
 What is this claim permitted to do, and what are its failure boundaries?
```

These questions are independent of domain.

They apply to mathematics, software, protocol design, symbolic notation, geometry, theology, ontology, and implementation.

A claim may be meaningful, inspiring, useful, or explanatory without being canonized for deterministic use.

---

## Canonization Pattern

A concept may be canonized only if it can be rewritten as at least one admissible structure:

```text
finite structure
 decidable, enumerable, bounded

algebraic structure
 operations, laws, morphisms

order structure
 partial order, total order, lattice, hierarchy

closure structure
 closure operator, fixed point, invariant reduction

boundary structure
 boundary invariant, d² = 0, separation condition

quotient structure
 equivalence class, canonical representative, normalization

duality structure
 chain/cochain, construction/evaluation, OMI/IMO pairing

decidable predicate
 algorithmically checkable yes/no condition

deterministic transition
 function, not uncontrolled relation

norm-preserving action
 isometry, invariant-preserving transformation
```

If a concept cannot be rewritten into one of these forms, it remains outside the canonization boundary.

It may still be preserved as:

```text
metaphor
research note
projection
diagram
interpretation
historical artifact
intuition
```

but it is not admissible as deterministic construction.

---

## Adjudication Pattern

Every claim must be assigned a proof-status level before use.

```text
P0 — Formally proven
 Fully verified in a proof assistant.
 No forbidden axioms, no admitted gaps.

P1 — Exhaustively checked
 Verified by finite computation, bounded search, or a decision procedure.

P2 — Mathematically modeled
 Stated formally with an informal proof or precise model.
 Not machine-checked.

P3 — Implementation precedent
 Works in practice or passes tests.
 Not yet connected to the formal spine.

P4 — Metaphor / visualization
 Useful for explanation only.
 Not proof.
```

### Use Permissions

```text
P0:
 may guide core use, architecture, implementation, and explanation

P1:
 may guide selected core use, architecture, implementation, and explanation

P2:
 may guide architecture and explanation;
 may guide implementation with caution;
 may not serve as core proof

P3:
 may guide implementation and explanation;
 may not guide core authority or architecture by itself

P4:
 may guide explanation only;
 may not guide core authority, architecture, or implementation
```

---

## Claim Registry

Every admitted claim must be recorded with an explicit registry entry.

```yaml
claim:
 id: unique.identifier
 name: Human-readable name
 source: origin or citation
 status: P0 | P1 | P2 | P3 | P4
 statement: formal or semi-formal statement
 module_or_file: location where the claim lives
 dependencies:
 - prerequisite.claim
 allowed_use:
 - what this claim may support
 forbidden_use:
 - what this claim must not be used to prove
 failure_boundary: what this claim does not establish
 promotion_path: how to raise the claim to a stronger status
```

The registry prevents the common collapse:

```text
not proved = false
```

The correct reading is:

```text
not proved = classify the claim, state its allowed use, and record its failure boundary
```

---

## Authority Boundary

Canonization and adjudication do not create a new authority layer.

They classify claims before use.

The boundary is:

```text
Proof
 canonizes admissible structure

Validation
 determines accepted state

Attestation
 witnesses accepted transition

Projection
 displays accepted state
```

Proof tells the system what structures may be trusted as deterministic.

Validation decides whether a specific candidate satisfies the accepted structure.

Attestation records that a transition occurred under the accepted rules.

Projection displays the accepted state or relation.

Projection does not retroactively prove the structure.

Implementation does not retroactively prove the theorem.

A metaphor does not become authority because it is useful.

---

## SETCO Placement

Inside SETCO, Canonization & Adjudication functions as the external reusable classification pattern.

```text
SETCO:
 ontology and research frame

Canonization:
 admission test for deterministic structure

Adjudication:
 permission and failure-boundary assignment

Omi-Canvas:
 spatial research framebuffer

Omnicron:
 resolver-facing interface

Omi-* faces:
 adaptive projection surfaces developed inside the frame
```

This makes SETCO a method for staging claims before they are allowed to become executable, architectural, canonical, or merely explanatory.

---

## Relation to OMI

In OMI terms, the pattern maps cleanly:

```text
P0:
 active proof theorem

P1:
 bounded exhaustive check or finite decision result

P2:
 defined model

P3:
 implemented precedent

P4:
 interpretation or projection
```

This corresponds to the operational distinction:

```text
rules:
 epistemic research objects

algorithms:
 implementation and execution objects
```

Rules may carry:

```text
KK / KU / UK / UU
```

Algorithms may carry:

```text
proved / implemented / defined_model / interpretation
```

The two systems should not be collapsed.

---

## Adapting the Pattern

To adopt this method in another project:

```text
1. Define the project’s proof-status ladder.
2. Define which structures are admissible for canonization.
3. Create a claim registry for every concept in use.
4. Annotate each use of a claim with its permitted level.
5. Record the claim’s failure boundary explicitly.
6. Define a promotion path for stronger status.
```

That is the method.

Everything else is domain-specific filling.

---

## Canon Statement

Canonization admits structure.

Adjudication assigns permission.

Validation accepts a candidate.

Attestation witnesses a transition.

Projection displays a result.

No layer may impersonate another.

A proved theorem is not an implementation.

A working implementation is not a proof.

A projection is not validation.

A metaphor is not authority.

SETCO preserves these distinctions so that research, explanation, implementation, and proof can cooperate without collapsing into one another.
:::

I would make this a **companion section** to the SETCO front matter, maybe titled:

```text
SETCO-METHOD.md
```

or inside the document as:

```text
Part II — Canonization & Adjudication
```

The strongest one-line lock is:

```text
SETCO is the frame; Canonization & Adjudication is the method for deciding what may enter the frame and what each claim is allowed to do once inside it.
```

`2026-07-12 23:26` | `id:260712-2326` ^260712-232652

Yes — this is the right move. I would add **Proof** and **Proposal** as faces, but I would not put them at the same semantic level as “algorithm” and “fact” without a clean type boundary.

The clean split becomes:

```text id="uzsteg"
Rules
 epistemic orientation

Facts
 stabilized claims

Algorithms
 executable/checkable procedures

Proofs
 authority/canonization support

Proposals
 candidate structures not yet admitted
```

Your earlier status model already separates research-facing rules from implementation-facing algorithms, with research modality `KK/KU/UK/UU` and implementation statuses like `proved`, `implemented`, `defined_model`, and `interpretation` filecite turn2file0 . The proof-book also separates proof, implementation, defined model, and interpretation so useful interpretations do not accidentally become theorems filecite turn3file0 .

So for SETCO pedagogy, I would use a **cube** rather than only a pyramid.

The pyramid is good for **assurance ascent**:

```text id="ikd4ys"
KK foundation
KU question
UK archive
UU frontier
```

But the cube is better for **pedagogy**, because you can assign vertices, edges, faces, and volumes.

:::writing{variant="document" id="95518"}
## SETCO Logic / Lambda Cube Pedagogy

SETCO may use a cube model to teach how claims move from proposal into canonized deterministic construction.

This cube is not the standard lambda cube as a formal equivalence claim. It is a SETCO pedagogical cube inspired by typed logic: objects, facts, algorithms, proofs, and proposals are kept distinct while their dependencies are made visible.

### Core Object Types

```text id="fkavsl"
Rule
 an epistemic orientation or governing sentence

Fact
 a stabilized claim or observed relation

Algorithm
 an executable, checkable, or implementable procedure

Proof
 a canonization support showing why a structure may be admitted

Proposal
 a candidate construction not yet admitted into canon
```

### Cube Reading

```text id="1mjfjq"
Vertices:
 individual claims, facts, rules, algorithms, proofs, or proposals

Edges:
 typed dependency relations

Faces:
 reusable classification planes

Interior:
 integrated SETCO construction space
```

### Suggested Faces

```text id="3xa662"
Epistemic Face
 KK / KU / UK / UU

Implementation Face
 proved / implemented / defined_model / interpretation

Canonization Face
 P0 / P1 / P2 / P3 / P4

Construction Face
 rule / fact / algorithm / proof / proposal

Projection Face
 impotent / innocuous / implementation-promoted

Interface Face
 omi-notation / omi-lisp / omi-port / Omi-* configuration
```

### Vertex Schema

```yaml id="cwqmos"
setco_vertex:
 id: omi.null_ring.closure
 kind: algorithm
 statement: "The Omicron Null Byte Omi-Ring closes by XOR witness reduction."

 epistemic:
 modality: KK

 implementation:
 status: proved

 adjudication:
 level: P0

 construction:
 type: algorithm
 supports:
 - omi.rule.address_identity
 - omi.fact.null_ring_edges

 interface:
 surface:
 - omi-notation
 - omnicron

 boundary:
 allowed_use:
 - deterministic closure reasoning
 - null gauge projection
 forbidden_use:
 - proving arbitrary runtime behavior
 - proving external physical claims
```

### Edge Schema

```yaml id="0iik6u"
setco_edge:
 from: omi.proposal.gnomic_ring
 to: omi.algorithm.null_ring_closure
 relation: "extends"
 edge_type: proposal_to_algorithm
 requirement: "must define gauge substitution and preservation boundary"
 status:
 epistemic_modality: KU
 adjudication_level: P2
```

### Pedagogical Rule

```text id="4be57d"
A proposal may point toward a proof.
A proof may canonize a structure.
A canonized structure may support an algorithm.
An algorithm may stabilize a fact.
A fact may support a rule.
A rule may govern future proposals.
```

### SETCO Cube Lock

```text id="axh8gv"
Pyramid:
 shows assurance ascent

Cube:
 shows typed relations among rules, facts, algorithms, proofs, and proposals

Tetrahedral frame:
 shows one Omi-* face as a local research/construction checkpoint

Logic/Lambda cube:
 teaches how claims depend on types, proofs, functions, and admissible construction
```

SETCO may therefore use both a pyramid and a cube.

The pyramid is the research method.

The cube is the pedagogy.

The tetrahedral frame is the Omi-* development face.

Omnicron reads those faces as resolver-facing surfaces.
:::

The sentence I would canonize is:

```text id="h202id"
SETCO uses the pyramid for epistemic assurance, the cube for typed pedagogy, and tetrahedral frames for developing Omi-* faces through Omnicron.
```

This keeps **proofs** and **proposals** visible without collapsing them into facts or algorithms.

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-12 23:33` | `id:260712-2333` ^260712-233331

Yes — **SETCO can become a hypercube graph methodology**. That is stronger than only pyramid/cube, because it lets you scale the pedagogy:

```text
pyramid = assurance ascent
cube = typed pedagogy
hypercube graph = reusable methodology for moving claims through many axes
```

The cube facts you listed fit perfectly as the **3D base case**:

```text
cube:
 8 vertices
 12 edges
 6 square faces
 Euler characteristic: V − E + F = 2
 Schläfli symbol: {4,3}
```

For SETCO, the important part is not the literal solid. It is the **cubical graph**: vertices are claim states, edges are lawful transitions, faces are classification planes, and higher-dimensional cubes add more independent axes.

Your uploaded OMI blackboard document already supports this because it frames OMI as a bounded citation workspace where knowledge sources contribute typed updates and a governor checks whether each contribution fits the bounded frame. It explicitly says OMI should use a Blackboard Pattern Architecture rather than P2P at the canon layer, with Omicron, Metatron, Tetragrammatron, Polybius, Delta, and projection agents acting as knowledge sources/governors inside a bounded workspace. filecite turn4file0 It also preserves the Latin-square rule that any two of attestation, attribution, and discovered point determine the third, which gives you the reversible edge logic for the graph. filecite turn4file0

Here is the canon-ready version:

:::writing{variant="document" id="58632"}
## SETCO as a Hypercube Graph Methodology

SETCO may be modeled as a hypercube graph methodology for classifying, developing, and adjudicating claims before they are admitted into deterministic construction.

The cube is the base pedagogical case.

```text
Cube:
 8 vertices
 12 edges
 6 square faces
 Euler characteristic: V − E + F = 2
 Schläfli symbol: {4,3}
```

In SETCO, the cube is not merely a geometric object. It is a claim-classification graph.

```text
Vertices:
 claim states

Edges:
 lawful transitions or dependencies

Faces:
 classification planes

Cells:
 integrated construction spaces

Hypercube:
 extension of the method across more independent axes
```

### Core Reading

```text
Pyramid:
 assurance ascent

Cube:
 typed pedagogy

Tetrahedral frame:
 local Omi-* development face

Hypercube graph:
 full SETCO methodology
```

The pyramid shows how research rises from foundation to frontier.

The cube shows how rules, facts, algorithms, proofs, and proposals relate.

The tetrahedral frame shows how one Omi-* face is developed.

The hypercube graph shows how multiple independent classification axes can be combined without collapsing into one authority.

---

## SETCO Hypercube Axes

A SETCO hypercube may include the following axes:

```text
Epistemic axis:
 KK / KU / UK / UU

Construction axis:
 rule / fact / algorithm / proof / proposal

Adjudication axis:
 P0 / P1 / P2 / P3 / P4

Implementation axis:
 proved / implemented / defined_model / interpretation

Projection axis:
 impotent / innocuous / implementation-promoted

Interface axis:
 omi-notation / omi-lisp / omi-port / Omi-* configuration

Dimensional axis:
 point / line / plane / space / hyperspace
```

Each axis adds one independent direction of classification.

The purpose of the hypercube is to prevent category collapse.

```text
A proposal is not a proof.
A proof is not an implementation.
An implementation is not a theorem.
A projection is not authority.
A metaphor is not canon.
A resolver surface is not an application command.
```

---

## Vertex Model

Each vertex in the SETCO hypercube is a classified claim.

```yaml
setco_vertex:
 id: omi.null_ring.interface_rule
 name: Null Ring Interface Rule
 kind: algorithm

 epistemic:
 modality: KK

 construction:
 type: algorithm

 adjudication:
 level: P0_or_P1

 implementation:
 status: proved_or_implemented

 projection:
 mode:
 - impotent
 - innocuous

 interface:
 surface:
 - Omnicron
 - omi-notation
 - omi-lisp
 - omi-port

 dimensional:
 role: point_to_space_projection

 allowed_use:
 - explain Omnicron return surfaces
 - classify Null Gauge and Gnomic Gauge projections

 forbidden_use:
 - replacing the Omi Object Model
 - commanding application behavior
 - proving unrelated runtime effects
```

---

## Edge Model

An edge records a lawful movement between classified states.

```yaml
setco_edge:
 from: proposal.gnomic_gauge_ring
 to: defined_model.gnomic_gauge_ring
 relation: promotes_to
 requirement:
 - define gauge substitution
 - define failure boundary
 - prove or check projection safety
```

Edges may represent:

```text
proposes
refines
implements
proves
depends_on
projects_to
interfaces_with
promotes_to
rejects_for_this_frame
```

An edge is not just a line. It is a typed transition.

---

## Face Model

A face is a two-axis plane where claims can be compared.

Examples:

```text
Rule × Proof:
 which rules have proof support?

Algorithm × Implementation:
 which algorithms are only defined, tested, or proved?

Proposal × Adjudication:
 which proposals are P2, P3, or P4 only?

Interface × Projection:
 which surfaces are impotent, innocuous, or implementation-promoted?

Fact × Epistemic:
 which facts are Known Known, Known Unknown, Unknown Known, or Unknown Unknown?
```

Faces allow SETCO to inspect local consistency without pretending the whole hypercube has been solved.

---

## Relation to OMI Blackboard

The SETCO hypercube can sit above the OMI blackboard.

```text
SETCO Hypercube:
 classifies claims and their authority

OMI Blackboard:
 stages bounded citation contributions

Omnicron:
 exposes resolver-facing surfaces

Tetragrammatron:
 governs bounded closure

Metatron:
 transposes and records interpolation

Omicron:
 supplies the null closure frame

Omi-* faces:
 configure adaptive interfaces
```

The OMI blackboard stores bounded contribution fragments.

The SETCO hypercube classifies what those fragments are allowed to mean.

The blackboard asks:

```text
Does this contribution fit the bounded frame?
```

The hypercube asks:

```text
What kind of claim is this, and what is it allowed to do?
```

Together, they form the method.

---

## Canon Lock

SETCO is a hypercube graph methodology.

It uses:

```text
vertices
 to hold classified claims

edges
 to record typed transitions

faces
 to compare classification planes

cells
 to stage integrated construction spaces

hypercube extensions
 to add new independent axes without collapsing old ones
```

The cube is the first complete pedagogical solid.

The hypercube is the scalable research method.

The Omi-* face is a local development frame.

Omnicron is the resolver-facing space through which those frames become inspectable.

Projection remains impotent or innocuous unless promoted by a separate implementation authority.
:::

The strongest one-line version:

```text
SETCO is a hypercube graph methodology for developing Omi-* faces: vertices classify claims, edges record lawful transitions, faces expose authority boundaries, and Omnicron resolves the resulting surfaces without collapsing them into application behavior.
```

I would carry that forward.

---

Yes. That is the right architectural move.

I would **move OMI Blackboard out of “OMI core” and into SETCO** as the **classification/categorization adapter layer** between external languages/tools and the pure Omi Object Model.

That keeps the base clean:

```text
Omi Object Model
 pure notation / pure object-address model

SETCO Blackboard Canvas
 external classification, categorization, staging, and adapter workspace

External languages/tools
 Coq / Haskell / TypeScript / C / Python / firmware / browsers / etc.
```

This also fixes the old tension around “blackboard architecture.” Your blackboard document already says OMI should stop before application behavior: OMI declares notation, defines citations, enables discovery, and projects only bounded innocuous or impotent surfaces. It explicitly reframes the blackboard as a bounded citation workspace rather than a P2P application protocol. filecite turn4file0

So I would define it like this:

:::writing{variant="document" id="85403"}
## SETCO Blackboard Canvas

### Omi Object Model Classification and Categorization Adapter

The **SETCO Blackboard Canvas** is the external classification and categorization adapter for the **Omi Object Model**.

It is not the OMI core.

It is not an application runtime.

It is not a new authority layer.

It is the external workspace where claims, rules, facts, algorithms, proofs, proposals, code artifacts, language bindings, and projection faces are staged before they are lowered into or interfaced with pure OMI notation.

```text
External Languages / Tools
 Coq
 Haskell
 TypeScript
 C
 Python
 firmware
 browser runtimes
 external applications
 ↓
SETCO Blackboard Canvas
 classification
 categorization
 claim staging
 proof/proposal/fact/algorithm separation
 hypercube graph workspace
 adapter mapping
 ↓
Omi Object Model
 pure omi-notation
 omi-lisp declarations
 omi-port declarations
 ↓
Bytecoded / Opcoded OMI Workspace
 assembler
 compiler
 resolver
 projection
```

The SETCO Blackboard Canvas functions as an OSI-like adapter model for OMI.

It separates external implementation languages from the pure Omi Object Model so that no language, runtime, proof assistant, compiler, or application accidentally becomes the authority of OMI itself.

---

## Boundary

The **Omi Object Model** remains the pure base:

```text
omi-notation
omi-lisp
omi-port
```

The **SETCO Blackboard Canvas** classifies and categorizes how external systems relate to that base.

```text
Coq
 proof surface

Haskell
 type/model surface

TypeScript
 browser/interface surface

C
 runtime/ISA/firmware surface

Python
 tooling/prototyping surface

External applications
 downstream application surfaces
```

These surfaces may adapt to OMI.

They do not define OMI.

---

## Hypercube Graph Workspace

The SETCO Blackboard Canvas uses a bytecoded or opcoded **Hypercube Graph Workspace**.

In that workspace:

```text
vertices
 classified claims, rules, facts, algorithms, proofs, proposals, adapters, and surfaces

edges
 typed transitions, dependencies, promotions, projections, and lowering paths

faces
 classification planes such as proof status, implementation status, epistemic modality, interface type, and projection mode

cells
 integrated construction regions where Omi-* faces are developed

hypercube extensions
 additional independent axes added without collapsing prior distinctions
```

The workspace exists to prevent category collapse.

```text
Coq proof ≠ C implementation
Haskell type ≠ runtime behavior
TypeScript projection ≠ validation
Python tool ≠ canon
omi-lisp declaration ≠ application command
omi-port declaration ≠ external connection
projection ≠ authority
```

---

## Adapter Role

The SETCO Blackboard Canvas adapts external systems to OMI through classification, not through application control.

```text
External artifact
 → classify
 → categorize
 → assign status
 → assign failure boundary
 → map to omi-notation / omi-lisp / omi-port
 → lower through assembler or compiler if permitted
 → expose through Omnicron if projection-safe
```

The adapter may ask:

```text
Is this a rule?
Is this a fact?
Is this an algorithm?
Is this a proof?
Is this a proposal?
Is this a projection?
Is this an implementation precedent?
Is this pure notation?
Is this an omi-lisp declaration?
Is this an omi-port declaration?
```

Only after classification may the object be staged for lowering.

---

## Relation to Omnicron

Omnicron is the resolver-facing interface.

The SETCO Blackboard Canvas prepares and classifies the surfaces that Omnicron may expose.

```text
SETCO Blackboard Canvas
 stages and classifies Omi-* faces

Omnicron
 resolves and exposes projection-safe surfaces

Omi Object Model
 remains the pure notation base
```

Omnicron may expose:

```text
omi-notation
 direct canonical notation surface

omi-lisp
 local declaration surface

omi-port
 external/adaptive port declaration surface

Omi-* faces
 configured interface surfaces from the SETCO Blackboard Canvas
```

If no prior address is available, Omnicron may return:

```text
(NULL . NULL)
```

or the corresponding gauge projection:

```text
F0 00 1C 1D 1E 1F 20 F0
```

If a declared interpretive gauge exists, Omnicron may expose:

```text
FΔ 00 1C 1D 1E 1F 20 FΔ
```

---

## Compiler / Assembler Path

The SETCO Blackboard Canvas may support an OMI assembler or compiler path.

```text
pure omi-notation
 → parse

omi-lisp
 → declare

omi-port
 → declare external/adaptive surface

SETCO Blackboard Canvas
 → classify and categorize

Hypercube Graph Workspace
 → assign vertices, edges, faces, cells

Assembler / Compiler
 → lower into bytecode or opcode form

Omnicron
 → resolve projection surface

Tetragrammatron
 → validate bounded closure when required

Metatron
 → transpose or interpolate without changing decision

Projection
 → display as impotent or innocuous surface
```

This path does not make the compiler the authority.

The compiler lowers classified structure.

The assembler encodes classified structure.

The authority boundary remains separate.

---

## Canon Lock

The **SETCO Blackboard Canvas** is the Omi Object Model classification and categorization adapter.

It is an OSI-like external model for connecting external applications, programming languages, proof systems, type systems, runtime code, and tooling to the pure Omi Object Model.

It uses a bytecoded or opcoded Hypercube Graph Workspace to stage Omi-* faces before they are lowered, assembled, compiled, resolved, or projected.

The pure Omi Object Model remains:

```text
omi-notation
omi-lisp
omi-port
```

The SETCO Blackboard Canvas adapts external surfaces to OMI.

Omnicron resolves those surfaces.

Tetragrammatron validates bounded closure.

Metatron transposes without changing the decision.

Projection displays only impotent or innocuous surfaces unless a separate implementation authority promotes further action.

External applications begin downstream.
:::

The concise lock is:

```text
SETCO Blackboard Canvas is the OSI-like classification adapter between external languages/applications and the pure Omi Object Model. It stages Omi-* faces in a hypercube graph workspace so Omnicron can resolve them without letting any external language, compiler, or application become OMI authority.
```

---

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-13 00:13` | `id:260713-0013` ^260713-001302

Yes — we should **reclassify/re-calibrate**, but not by moving everything wholesale.

The clean split is:

```text
Omi Object Model
 owns the pure canonical object/address model:
 omi-notation, omi-lisp, omi-port, bounded envelopes, null rings, gauges, observers

SETCO
 owns the methodology and adapter workspace:
 classification, categorization, canonization, adjudication, blackboard canvas,
 hypercube graph pedagogy, proof/proposal/fact/algorithm sorting
```

So the answer is:

```text
omi-axioms proof book:
 mostly belongs to Omi Object Model as proof authority rail,
 but its adjudication/canonization method should be mirrored into SETCO.

omi-isa:
 belongs to Omi Object Model as implementation/architecture/runtime rail.

omi-os:
 should not be OMI core.
 It should be classified as a SETCO / Omnicron-facing operating surface
 or downstream implementation shell over OMI.
```

## Reclassification Map

| Artifact | Primary home | SETCO relationship |
|---|---|---|
| `omi-axioms` Coq theorems | **Omi Object Model** | SETCO reads them through canonization/adjudication |
| OMI deterministic proof book | **Omi Object Model** | SETCO may use it as the proof-status source |
| `PROOF-ADJUDICATION.md` / P0–P4 | **SETCO method**, mirrored in `omi-axioms` | This is the reusable classification/adjudication pattern |
| `CANONIZATION-ADJUDICATION.md` | **SETCO** | External reusable method for classifying claims |
| `omi-isa` VM / ISA / C runtime | **Omi Object Model implementation rail** | SETCO categorizes it as implementation/runtime surface |
| `omi-os` | **SETCO / Omnicron-facing surface**, not core | OS-like application/adapter layer over OMI |
| OMI Blackboard Canvas | **SETCO** | Classification/categorization adapter for external tools/languages |

The reason is already present in your uploaded docs. The `omi-axioms` README says it is the “proof authority rail for OMI” and that it does not render, execute, or accept runtime state; it proves bounded invariants that canon may name, OMI-Lisp may declare, OMI-ISA may witness, and receipts may record. filecite turn5file7 That means `omi-axioms` should stay attached to the **Omi Object Model**, not become SETCO itself.

But the same repo also already contains `docs/CANONIZATION-ADJUDICATION.md`, described as an external-facing pattern for how to classify claims. filecite turn5file7 That part is **SETCO method**.

## Recommended Lock

```text
OMI owns the object model.
SETCO owns the classification method.

omi-axioms proves OMI invariants.
SETCO adjudicates what those proofs, proposals, facts, and implementations are allowed to do.

omi-isa implements and witnesses OMI behavior.
SETCO classifies omi-isa artifacts by status, boundary, and interface role.

omi-os is not OMI core.
omi-os is an Omnicron-facing operating surface over OMI, staged through the SETCO Blackboard Canvas.
```

## How to recalibrate `omi-axioms`

Do **not** rename `omi-axioms` into SETCO.

Instead add a front-matter clarification:

```markdown
# omi-axioms

`omi-axioms` is the proof authority rail for the Omi Object Model.

It supplies formally checked and proof-adjudicated structures that SETCO may classify, stage, and reference through its canonization/adjudication method.

`omi-axioms` does not execute runtime state, render projections, or define application behavior.

SETCO may reuse the proof-status ladder, claim registry, and canonization/adjudication method, but the active theorem authority remains in `omi-axioms`.
```

This matches the proof-spine rule that only modules listed in `_CoqProject`, compiled by `make proof-strict`, and accepted by `coqchk` are part of the proof authority rail. filecite turn5file10

## How to recalibrate `omi-isa`

Do **not** rename `omi-isa` into SETCO either.

`omi-isa` is the artifact/runtime memory of the Omi Object Model. Its implementation doc says implementation is where narrative becomes executable: if code does not run, architecture is only a proposal; if tests do not pass, behavior is not stable; if proof does not compile, theorem is not established. filecite turn5file1

So classify it as:

```text
omi-isa:
 Omi Object Model runtime / ISA / VM / envelope / transport implementation rail

SETCO role:
 implementation surface classified by the SETCO Blackboard Canvas
```

Suggested front-matter:

```markdown
# omi-isa

`omi-isa` is the implementation and runtime witness rail for the Omi Object Model.

It contains the C runtime, ISA, VM, envelope transport, dispatch, observer surfaces, tests, WASM/browser surfaces, and firmware experiments.

SETCO may classify `omi-isa` artifacts as algorithms, implementations, proofs targets, proposals, or projections, but SETCO does not replace the runtime authority of the artifacts.

The artifact either builds or it does not.
The test either passes or it does not.
The proof target either connects to `omi-axioms` or remains an implementation precedent.
```

That is consistent with the implementation document’s line: “Tests do not replace proofs. Proofs do not replace tests. They answer different questions.” filecite turn5file1

## Where `omi-os` should land

`omi-os` should be **SETCO-facing**, not OMI-core.

Reason: an OS implies scheduling, resources, application surfaces, device interaction, execution context, and policy. Your architecture doc says OMI architecture defines protocol surfaces like OMI-Lisp, compiler, ISA, VM, observers, 512-bit envelopes, dispatch, transport, mesh, and receipts, but it must not overclaim proof or let extensions alter canonical replay. filecite turn5file5

So:

```text
omi-os:
 Omnicron-facing operating surface
 staged by SETCO Blackboard Canvas
 implemented over Omi Object Model primitives
 downstream from pure OMI notation/lisp/port
```

Not:

```text
omi-os:
 root OMI authority
```

## Final Structure

```text
SETCO
 methodology
 canonization/adjudication
 blackboard canvas
 hypercube graph workspace
 classification/categorization adapter
 omi-os / Omnicron-facing operating surfaces

Omi Object Model
 pure omi-notation
 omi-lisp
 omi-port
 null/gauge rings
 envelope structure
 observer model
 Omicron / Metatron / Tetragrammatron / Omnicron roles

omi-axioms
 proof authority rail for OMI invariants

omi-isa
 implementation/runtime witness rail for OMI artifacts
```

The best canon sentence is:

```text
SETCO classifies and stages; OMI defines the object model; omi-axioms proves admissible invariants; omi-isa witnesses executable behavior; omi-os, if retained, is an Omnicron-facing operating surface over OMI, not the OMI core.
```

Yes — I think **new SETCO repos are the right move**, and I would prefer the shorter names:

rather than:

```text
setco-omnicron-canvas
setco-omnicron-os
```

Because **Omnicron should be a role inside the framework**, not part of every repo name. If every repo says `setco-omnicron-*`, the names imply Omnicron owns SETCO. Cleaner boundary:

```text
SETCO
 independent transition / classification framework

Omnicron
 resolver-facing role inside the OMI/SETCO bridge

Omi Object Model
 pure declarative computational paradigm
```

Your existing docs already support this split: `omi-axioms` is described as the proof authority rail for OMI, while canonization/adjudication is an external-facing classification pattern that SETCO can own. filecite turn5file7 And `omi-isa` is the runtime/artifact rail where code, tests, WebAssembly, firmware, and delegated proofs become accountable artifacts, not the classification framework itself. filecite turn5file1

## Recommended repo split

```text
setco
 root framework / manifesto / method / cross-repo map

setco-blackboard
 classification and categorization workspace

setco-canvas
 visual / graph / hypercube / tetrahedral frame surface

setco-os
 operating surface / IDE shell / adapter environment
```

Then OMI stays clean:

```text
omi-canon
 canonical language and doctrine

omi-axioms
 Coq proof authority rail

omi-isa
 C runtime / ISA / VM / envelope / firmware rail

omi-object-model
 optional future root repo if you want one clear public landing page
```

## How I would define each SETCO repo

:::writing{variant="document" id="52891"}
# SETCO Repository Boundary

## `setco`

`setco` is the independent transition framework for separating, classifying, and loosely connecting modern computational environments to the Omi Object Model.

It defines the method.

It does not replace OMI.

It explains how claims, proofs, proposals, implementations, projections, and runtime artifacts move through classification before they are allowed to influence deterministic construction.

```text
SETCO:
 Systematic Epistemic Theological Computational Ontology

Role:
 independent classification, transition, and pedagogy framework
```

---

## `setco-blackboard`

`setco-blackboard` is the classification and categorization workspace.

It stages claims before they enter or interface with OMI.

It owns:

```text
claim registry
canonization/adjudication
P0–P4 proof ladder
rules / facts / algorithms / proofs / proposals
failure boundaries
promotion paths
external language adapters
```

It answers:

```text
What kind of thing is this?
What is it allowed to do?
What must it not be used for?
How can it be promoted?
```

It is the SETCO-side blackboard, not the OMI core.

---

## `setco-canvas`

`setco-canvas` is the visual and structural graph surface.

It owns:

```text
hypercube graph methodology
tetrahedral Omi-* development frames
KK / KU / UK / UU research modality
vertices / edges / faces / cells
claim-state diagrams
pedagogical projection maps
```

It answers:

```text
Where does this claim sit?
What is connected to it?
Which face is being developed?
Which edge represents the next transition?
```

This repo makes SETCO teachable.

---

## `setco-os`

`setco-os` is the operating surface or IDE-like shell.

It owns:

```text
workspace orchestration
external tool integration
language adapters
project navigation
compiler / assembler front ends
Omnicron-facing resolver surface
developer experience
```

It answers:

```text
How does a human or agent work with SETCO?
How do Coq, Haskell, TypeScript, C, Python, and other tools plug in?
How are OMI declarations inspected, staged, assembled, compiled, or projected?
```

`setco-os` is not OMI authority.

It is the operating environment around SETCO and OMI.
:::

## Why short names are better

Use:

```text
setco-blackboard
setco-canvas
setco-os
```

Because these repos can serve more than OMI later.

Then inside each repo, define the OMI bridge:

```text
SETCO → Omnicron → Omi Object Model
```

That lets SETCO become a general transition framework while still making OMI its first and deepest application.

## Final architecture lock

```text
setco
 independent transition framework

setco-blackboard
 classification / adjudication / promotion workspace

setco-canvas
 hypercube graph / tetrahedral pedagogy surface

setco-os
 IDE-like operating surface and adapter shell

omi-canon
 OMI language and doctrine

 OMI proof authority rail

omi-isa
 OMI executable artifact rail
```

The canon sentence:

```text
SETCO is the independent transition framework that classifies, stages, and teaches how modern computational environments can loosely connect to the Omi Object Model without becoming its authority.
```

That gives you the bigger public bridge: **SETCO explains the transition; OMI is the new computational paradigm being transitioned toward.**
